summary: Cantrill discovers that NetApp's original 1992 business plan explicitly targeted Sun Microsystems with the same differentiators—high availability, easy administration, high performance, and low price—that Sun's Fishworks team is now using to target NetApp. The irony is that the company NetApp disrupted is now disrupting NetApp using nearly the same playbook. Cantrill argues this illustrates a fundamental truth: economics always wins, and no company can sustain itself against unfavorable economic forces indefinitely.
key_points: NetApp's 1992 business plan specifically targeted Sun as a slower, more expensive competitor
NetApp's criticisms of Sun then mirror Sun's Fishworks criticisms of NetApp now
The same disruption playbook is being used against the original disruptor
Economic forces ultimately determine technology outcomes regardless of current market position
money_quotes: It is stunning the degree to which NetApp's own business plan – nearly verbatim – is being used against it, not least by the very company that NetApp originally disrupted.

NetApp's success with their original business plan and our nascent success with Fishworks point to the most important lesson that the history of technology has to teach: economics always wins – a product or a technology or a company ultimately cannot prop up unsustainable economics.

Perhaps unlike Hitz, however, I had to learn that lesson the hard way: in the post-bubble meltdown that brought Sun within an inch of its life. But then again, perhaps Hitz has yet to have his final lesson on the subject…
The culture among software engineers at Sun places little value on performance.
key_insight: Market disruptors eventually become the disrupted, as the economic forces that enabled their rise can be wielded against them by hungrier competitors using the same playbook.
